title: Platinum Hypersensitivity and Toxicity -- Pathophysiology
content: The neurotoxic effects of platinum compounds exist through separate mechanisms. The neuronal damage caused by cisplatin and oxaliplatin occurs due to their ability to accumulate in the dorsal root ganglia, leading to nucleolar formation and the suppression of essential neuronal maintenance genes. [17] The rapid neurotoxic effects of oxaliplatin arise from its ability to bind extracellular calcium ions, which modifies sodium channel conductance properties and generates neuronal excitability. [18] The toxic effects of platinum compounds on the ears result from cochlear hair cell death due to their ability to accumulate. The production of reactive oxygen species, combined with glutathione depletion, induces lipid peroxidation and calcium dysregulation, ultimately leading to the apoptosis of hair cells. [19]

title: Opsoclonus -- Toxicokinetics -- Mechanisms of Toxicity Leading to Opsoclonus
content: Opsoclonus caused by toxins and drugs arises from various neurotoxic mechanisms that disrupt cerebellar and brainstem function. Lithium alters calcium signaling and inhibits Purkinje cell activity, leading to impaired motor control. Metronidazole induces axonal swelling in cerebellar pathways and disrupts γ-aminobutyric acid (GABA) transmission, contributing to abnormal eye movements. Platinum-based chemotherapeutic agents such as cisplatin and carboplatin trigger oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, and Purkinje cell apoptosis. Aminoglycosides, including gentamicin and tobramycin, damage vestibulocerebellar pathways, resulting in opsoclonus and ataxia. Heavy metals such as lead, mercury, and arsenic interfere with neurotransmission, promote oxidative injury, and impair cerebellar function, further compounding neurotoxicity.

title: Cisplatin -- Mechanism of Action
content: The drug is primarily excreted in the urine, with 10% in the bile. Its initial half-life is approximately 20 to 30 minutes, with a terminal half-life of 24 hours. ALbumin-bound platinum undergoes slow administration of minimally five days.

title: Physiology, Vestibular System -- Clinical Significance
content: Salicylate (aspirin) can reversibly eliminate outer hair cell electromotility to induce hearing loss. Salicylate competitively binds to the motor protein reversibly inhibiting electromotility. Other symptoms of aspirin toxicity include nausea, vomiting, tinnitus, hyperpnea, and disorientation. Other common ototoxic agents include aminoglycosides, furosemide, cisplatin, and quinines. [14]

title: Ethacrynic Acid -- Contraindications
content: Ethacrynic acid is contraindicated in patients already receiving treatment with other ototoxic drugs such as cisplatin or gentamycin. When taken alone, ototoxic drugs like gentamycin have a relatively low uptake into the cochlea, explaining why they rarely cause hearing loss. However, when taken with high doses of ethacrynic acid, permanent hearing loss can be observed. This effect appears to be caused by ethacrynic acid's effect on cochlear tight junctions. Damage to these tight junctions leads to increased permeability and a weakened blood-cochlear barrier allowing entry of ototoxic drugs. [5]

title: Platinum Hypersensitivity and Toxicity -- Etiology
content: The 3 main platinum compounds—cisplatin, carboplatin, and oxaliplatin—differ in their chemical structures, clinical applications, adverse effects, and indications for treatment. These differences include variations in hypersensitivity incidence (5%-10% for cisplatin, 9%-27% for carboplatin, and 10%-24% for oxaliplatin), primary dose-limiting toxicities, and onset patterns of adverse reactions, which are crucial for clinical decision-making and management approaches. [5] At the same time, platinum compounds produce a set of dose-limiting organ toxicities that affect nearly all major body systems. The dose-limiting toxicity of cisplatin is nephrotoxicity, which is observed in 20% to 30% of patients despite the use of adequate hydration regimens. [6] In contrast , carboplatin has reduced nephrotoxicity but is associated with higher rates of myelosuppression and thrombocytopenia, affecting approximately 40% of patients. [6] Additional adverse effects that impact patients' quality of life include neurotoxicity and ototoxicity. [7]

title: Cisplatin -- Toxicity
content: Ototoxicity - Monitoring for ototoxicity includes assessing the patient for ringing in the ears, high-frequency hearing loss, and decreased ability to follow conversations. Deafness has been reported but is not a common effect of cisplatin use. Loss of hearing acuity can be detrimental to language development in pediatric populations. [25] [35] Gonadotoxicity - Cisplatin is toxic to the gonads; it can cause impairment of spermatogenesis and dose-dependent ovarian failure leading to premature menopause. [25]

title: Audiogram Interpretation -- Normal and Critical Findings -- Ototoxicity
content: Ototoxicity may cause sensorineural hearing loss by damaging various inner ear structures, such as the cochlea's inner hair cells. Common causes include platinum-based chemotherapeutic agents, aminoglycosides, anti-malarial agents, antineoplastic drugs, loop diuretics, and high dosages of non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs. The audiogram pattern for ototoxicity is often similar to presbycusis, with a bilateral sensorineural hearing loss more prominent in the high frequencies. [15]

title: Cisplatin -- Continuing Education Activity
content: Objectives: Identify the mechanism of action of cisplatin. Describe the adverse effects associated with cisplatin therapy. Review the appropriate monitoring necessary with cisplatin therapy. Outline how collaboration and coordination among the interprofessional team can enhance patient care when dosing and monitoring cisplatin to improve patient outcomes for patients receiving antineoplastic therapy with cisplatin. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

title: Nephrotoxic Medications -- Mechanism of Action -- Tubular Cell Toxicity
content: The presence of certain pharmaceutical agents may damage the cells of the proximal tubules via free radical formation, mitochondrial damage, and transport systems damage. These agents include aminoglycosides, amphotericin B, adefovir, cisplatin, and foscarnet. [5]

title: Platinum Hypersensitivity and Toxicity -- Etiology
content: Organ-specific platinum toxicities follow epidemiological patterns that demonstrate complex characteristics. The incidence of cisplatin-induced nephrotoxicity ranges from 20% to 30% in patients, despite current hydration protocols, with higher rates observed in older patients and those with pre-existing renal impairment. [6] The occurrence of neurotoxicity differs significantly between agents and depends on the cumulative dose administered. [6] Cisplatin is associated with cumulative sensory neuropathy, affecting 30% to 40% of patients. In contrast, oxaliplatin induces acute cold-induced dysesthesias in approximately 90% of patients during infusion and causes cumulative sensory neurotoxicity in 50% of patients who receive more than 780 to 850 mg/m². [8]

title: Seminoma -- Complications
content: Ototoxicity: Cisplatin can cause permanent, sensorineural hearing loss and tinnitus.

title: Cisplatin -- Mechanism of Action
content: Cisplatin acts via non-cell cycle-specific cytotoxicity, which is achieved through the covalent binding of platinum to the purine bases guanine and adenine. This covalent binding leads to intra-strand and inter-strand crosslinks causing subsequent strand breaks. While DNA repair mechanisms are at play, cells often undergo apoptotic or non-apoptotic cell death due to remnant damaged DNA, RNA, and proteins. [19] Cisplatin chemotherapy is particularly effective at targeting rapidly dividing cells, as appreciated in rapidly growing malignant tumors. [20] Theoretically, cisplatin may not be as useful for slow-growing tumors.

title: Cisplatin -- Toxicity
content: CIsplatin has several black box warnings, including nephrotoxicity, peripheral neuropathy, severe nausea and vomiting, and myelosupression.

title: Tinnitus -- Toxicokinetics
content: Many modern antineoplastic drugs are ototoxic, including bleomycin, cis-platinum, methotrexate, and bumetanide. These cause hearing loss and tinnitus that may not be reversible. Ethacrynic acid, acetazolamide, are diuretics listed as ototoxic Aspirin in higher doses causes tinnitus. Fortunately, this is reversible. Tinnitus has been reported with other NSAID medications.

title: Cinchonism -- Toxicokinetics
content: Quinoline derivatives have a multitude of side effects, including neural, retinal, gastrointestinal, and auditory toxicity. Quinoline derivatives are toxic to the auditory system, presumably due to the disruption of cochlear hair cells and the blockade of ion channels of neurons in the auditory system, causing hearing loss and tinnitus. In addition, it causes ataxia, tremor, and dystonic reactions. In a mouse study, a dopaminergic pathway to assess these symptoms was explored. It was found that quinine depolarized resting membrane potential gives a possible underlying mechanism for the movement disorders of cinchonism as well as a role in hearing loss and tinnitus. [1]

title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2212
content: Alkylating agents are classified under the category of pharmacological effects—molecular mechanisms, indicating that their therapeutic action is understood within this mechanistic framework.

title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1920
content: Cisplatin, lomustine, mechlorethamine, vincristine sulfate, chloromethine hydrochloride, cyclophosphamide anhydrous, liposomal doxorubicin hydrochloride, procarbazine hydrochloride, ifosfamide, streptozocin, and vinblastine sulfate are all chemotherapeutic agents that may be used to treat Hodgkin disease (مرض هودجكين). These drugs represent a variety of classes—including platinum compounds, alkylating agents, antimetabolites, anthracyclines, and vinca alkaloids—and are commonly incorporated into multi‑drug regimens to improve response rates and reduce the risk of relapse. Their combined use exploits different mechanisms of action, such as DNA cross‑linking, inhibition of nucleic acid synthesis, and disruption of microtubule formation, thereby enhancing overall therapeutic efficacy against Hodgkin lymphoma. Selecting appropriate agents and dosing schedules is essential for optimizing patient outcomes while managing potential toxicities.

title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1437
content: In radiation biology, each qualifier can modulate, enhance, mitigate, or otherwise influence physiological responses to ionizing and non‑ionizing radiation. Hypoxanthine’s PARP‑inhibitory activity specifically suppresses DNA‑repair mechanisms, affecting cellular recovery after radiation‑induced damage. Recognizing these agents as radiation modifiers informs the design of protective strategies, therapeutic regimens, and risk assessments for both therapeutic radiation exposure and accidental radiation incidents. Collectively, the inclusion of these permissible qualifiers facilitates standardized manufacturing, regulatory compliance, targeted therapeutic development, and a comprehensive understanding of immunological, metabolic, and radiobiological processes, thereby improving patient care and advancing biomedical research.

title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1415
content: Aminoglycoside antibacterials constitute a class of antibiotics that share a common mechanism of inhibiting bacterial protein synthesis. Within this class, streptomycin is recognized as a specific member, and micronomicin is also classified as an aminoglycoside antibacterial. Both streptomycin and micronomicin inherit the characteristic properties of aminoglycoside antibacterials, including activity against aerobic gram‑negative organisms and the potential for nephrotoxic and ototoxic side effects. Understanding that streptomycin and micronomicin belong to the broader aminoglycoside antibacterial group helps clinicians select appropriate therapy, monitor for class‑related adverse effects, and appreciate the shared pharmacologic profile of these agents.

title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1590
content: Decreased Mitosis is identified as a physiologic effect linked to oxytetracyklin, indicating that exposure to oxytetracyklin can suppress cellular division. Meclocycline is mapped to oxytetracyklin, reflecting that meclocycline is closely related to oxytetracyklin, likely sharing similar tetracycline‑class properties and mechanisms of action. Together, these relationships suggest that both oxytetracyklin and its related compound meclocycline may exert antiproliferative effects through the inhibition of mitosis, a consideration relevant to their therapeutic use and potential side‑effect profiles.

title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 4 -- Community 2712
content: Alkylating agents constitute a class of chemotherapeutic drugs that exert their antineoplastic effect by adding alkyl groups to DNA, causing cross‑linking and ultimately cell death. Mitomycin is an example of an alkylating agent, illustrating the typical properties of this drug class. Because agents within this class share similar mechanisms of action and overlapping toxicities, the concurrent use of cyclophosphamide—a drug also belonging to the alkylating agent category—with other alkylating agents is generally contraindicated. This contraindication reflects the need to avoid additive adverse effects, such as heightened bone‑marrow suppression and organ toxicity, when multiple alkylating agents are administered together. Clinicians therefore carefully consider drug selection and avoid combining cyclophosphamide with other alkylating agents like mitomycin to ensure safer patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 227
content: rather than a therapeutic action. Together, these interrelationships clarify how drug classes, individual agents, chemical families, therapeutic uses, mechanisms of action, contraindications and adverse‑effect profiles are organized, supporting clinicians in selecting appropriate therapies, anticipating co‑occurring risks and monitoring relevant clinical outcomes.

title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 531
content: Conventional alkylating and antimetabolite chemotherapies—chlorambucil, cyclophosphamide anhydrous, ifosfamide, mechlorethamine, procarbazine (including its hydrochloride form), dakarbazin and lomustine—exert cytotoxic effects on rapidly dividing Hodgkin lymphoma cells. Platinum‑based therapy with cisplatin and anthracycline antibiotics doxorubicin hydrochloride (both standard and liposomal formulations) add DNA‑damaging mechanisms. Additional cytotoxic agents used in Hodgkin protocols include bleomycin sulfate, vinblastine sulfate, vinorelbine tartrate and streptozocin, each targeting distinct aspects of tumor proliferation and survival. Targeted and immunomodulatory drugs expand therapeutic options: brentuximab vedotin delivers an anti‑CD30 antibody‑drug conjugate directly to Hodgkin lymphoma cells; pembrolizumab, a PD‑1 checkpoint inhibitor, enhances antitumor immune responses; and sargramostim, a recombinant granulocyte‑macrophage colony‑stimulating factor, can augment host

title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1613
content: Chlorambucil, cisplatin, mechlorethamine, brentuximab vedotin, cyclophosphamide anhydrous, liposomal doxorubicin hydrochloride, procarbazine hydrochloride, ifosfamide, streptozocin, and vinorelbine tartrate are all agents that may be used to treat مرض هودجكين (Hodgkin disease). These drugs represent a range of chemotherapy and targeted‑therapy options that can be incorporated into therapeutic regimens for Hodgkin lymphoma, reflecting the disease’s responsiveness to multiple cytotoxic and immunologic mechanisms. Their potential use underscores the importance of individualized treatment planning and combination strategies in managing patients with Hodgkin disease.

title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1991
content: The mechanism of action for merkaptamin is currently described as an unknown cellular or molecular interaction, indicating that the precise cellular or molecular processes by which merkaptamin exerts its effects have not yet been elucidated.

title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1853
content: Vinca alkaloids and analogues, antineoplastic constitute a class of chemotherapy agents that includes the individual compounds vinblastin, vindesin, vinkristin and vinorelbin. Each of these agents belongs to the Vinca alkaloid family and functions as an antineoplastic drug, contributing to the overall therapeutic arsenal used to treat various malignancies. Recognizing that vinblastin, vindesin, vinkristin and vinorelbin are members of this broader Vinca alkaloid group helps clinicians understand their shared mechanisms of action and potential cross‑reactivity, facilitating more informed selection and management of cancer treatment regimens.

title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2740
content: PARP inhibitory agents are classified as hypoxanthine derivatives, indicating that they belong to the chemical class of hypoxanthine and function as inhibitors of poly(ADP‑ribose) polymerase. This classification highlights the structural relationship between PARP inhibitory compounds and the hypoxanthine scaffold, which is relevant for understanding their mechanism of action and potential therapeutic applications.

title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 65
content: subdivide into specific therapeutic products and formulations across dermatology, ophthalmology, otology, and oral care, informing clinicians about drug composition, therapeutic indications, and shared mechanisms of action, and linking bacterial meningitis to its etiologic agents.

title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2774
content: The ATC category “Other cytotoxic antibiotics” comprises agents that are used for their cytotoxic properties in cancer treatment, and both bleomycin and mithramycin are classified as members of this group. By being placed together in the “Other cytotoxic antibiotics in ATC” class, bleomycin and mithramycin are recognized as related cytotoxic antibiotics despite having distinct chemical structures and mechanisms of action, underscoring their shared role in oncologic pharmacotherapy.

title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1572
content: Chlorambucil, cisplatina, dakarbazin, mechlorethamin, prokarbazin, vincristine sulfate, cyclophosphamide anhydrous, doxorubicin hydrochloride, doxorubicin hydrochloride liposome, streptozocin, and vinorelbin tartarát are all reported to have potential therapeutic activity against مرض هودجكين (Hodgkin disease). These agents represent a diverse group of chemotherapeutic drugs—including alkylating agents, platinum‑based compounds, anthracyclines, vinca alkaloids, and other cytotoxic agents—that may be employed in treatment regimens for Hodgkin disease. Their varied mechanisms of action provide multiple options for disease control, particularly in cases that are refractory or relapsed, thereby expanding the therapeutic arsenal available to clinicians caring for patients with Hodgkin disease.

title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 576
content: The otologic therapeutic category “corticosteroids and antiinfectives in combination, otologic” comprises combination products that pair a corticosteroid with an anti‑infective agent for ear‑related conditions. Within this class, formulations containing fluocinolone acetonide together with an anti‑infective, as well as those containing prednisolone together with an anti‑infective, are recognized as members. Both fluocinolone acetonide‑antiinfective and prednisolone‑antiinfective preparations therefore belong to the broader group of corticosteroid‑antiinfective otologic combinations, reflecting their shared mechanism of delivering anti‑inflammatory and antimicrobial effects in the treatment of otologic disorders.
